titles.title: Toward Scalable Spatial Intelligence for Embodied AI: Language Grounding and 3D Reconstruction

abstracts.value: The long-term promise of embodied AI is a robot that can see, talk, and act in human environments. Realizing this promise hinges on two capabilities that connect perception and language at scale: 3D reconstruction, which builds a coherent spatial model of the world from images, and 3D–text grounding, which links natural language to specific objects, attributes, and relations in that model. This dissertation argues that the key missing ingredient for robust, everyday performance is scaling: scaling the semantics of how language is used, scaling the data that supervises grounding, and scaling the algorithms that reconstruct and update 3D state.First, we introduce LLM-Grounder, which treats a large language model as an agentic planner and evaluator for open–vocabulary 3D–text grounding. By decomposing complex, relational instructions and arbitrating among 3D candidates with spatial and commonsense reasoning, LLM-Grounder improves zero-shot grounding on long, compositional queries without additional task-specific labels.Second, to address the scarcity of dense supervision, we contribute 3D-GRAND, a million-scale resource with 6.2M noun-level, densely grounded 3D–text pairs across approximately 40K household scenes. We further release 3D-POPE, a benchmark for probing object hallucination in grounded 3D language, facilitating systematic evaluation of whether models respect 3D evidence rather than language priors. Together, these resources enable instruction tuning that more closely matches the richness of natural household dialogue and reveal clear positive scaling trends with data density and breadth.Third, we develop fast and scalable 3D reconstruction. Fast3R performs single-pass multi-view reconstruction, yielding over 300× speedups compared to pairwise + global–alignment pipelines while maintaining strong pose and geometry accuracy. We then extend reconstruction to thousand–view regimes with Fast3R-v2, which introduces (i) register fusion—summarizing per–view patch tokens into a compact set of learned registers to make global fusion far more efficient—and (ii) sequence parallelism, which partitions long multi-view sequences across multiple GPUs so that model capacity and throughput scale with available hardware.Across grounding and reconstruction, this thesis substantiates a scaling–centric view: when semantics (agentic reasoning), data (dense 3D–language supervision), and algorithms (architectures that exploit parallel hardware) are scaled together, embodied systems become markedly better at understanding and communicating about the 3D world. We discuss how these ingredients also position future extensions in dynamic (4D) reconstruction and world–model pretraining, moving toward unified and scalable spatial intelligence beyond the lab.

titles.title: &s;Hacking&s; Assistive Technology: Creating Personal AI Tools for Access

abstracts.value: This dissertation contributes a motivation for and approaches to &s;hack&s; assistive software: to move beyond one-size-fits all assistive technology design, and towards enabling anyone to create and customize AI tools for accessibility that meet their unique needs. AI-based assistive technologies have been increasingly adopted by blind people to gain visual access in scenarios such as reading printed text, identifying objects, and navigating. These services are typically designed to maximize broad applicability and usability, focusing on simple and common use cases under the assumption of universal needs. Yet, this results in inflexibility, leaving a long tail of diverse and situational needs that AI assistive technologies cannot account for. My dissertation argues that in addition to &s;lowering the floor&s; and making AI tools that are usable for everyone, we also need to &s;raise the ceiling&s; and create systems that enable people to create and do more. Building on a foundation of &s;making&s; and DIY cultures, I bring those philosophies into the assistive software space, and develop new approaches for people to make and adapt software to their needs.I first present work that studies the challenges with existing assistive applications, contributing evidence that blind people already put a significant amount of effort into working around gaps in these tools, and that they engage in design and making to do so, through three strategies which I term hacking, switching, and combining. I demonstrate that there is a significant gap between the assistive technology solutions that people desire to create, and the approaches that can support them in doing so. I then contribute two novel tools that leverage end-user programming approaches in order to close this gap. First, I present ProgramAlly, an end-user programming application for creating customized filters for visual information, using existing models as building blocks that can be rearranged. Next, I present AllyExtensions, a system leveraging mobile automation tools to augment already deployed applications with new features in a flexible way. Through designing and evaluating these systems, I demonstrate end-user creation processes that are approachable, accessible, and expressive for blind people, as in ProgramAlly, and that can also be applied practically to real-world assistive technology workflows, as in AllyExtensions. Overall, this dissertation presents motivation and methods for the democratization of assistive technology creation, and supports people in having greater control over AI-based technologies in their lives.

titles.title: CMOS Mixer-First Radios Using Wireless Signals Optimized for Ultra-Low Power Networks

abstracts.value: The Internet-of-Things (IoT) has been gaining popularity for both personal use and professional use. However, to keep up with this boom in demand, device operational lifetimes must be long as to prevent the need for constant user intervention (i.e., replacing batteries). Improvements in energy density for small form factor batteries has been very slow, not yielding the necessary requirements for envisioned IoT systems. The most promising alternative is to improve the electronics. In particular, wireless communication is typically the most power hungry part of an IoT device, so the reduction in power consumption for CMOS receivers and transmitters is crucial.In this work, low power wireless communication integrated circuits are designed with an emphasis on the mixer-first architectures. A theoretical framework is presented for mixer-first receivers, which have shown great promise as a balance between power and performance. Subsequently, an ultra-low power (ULP) FSK receiver is designed in GF12nm FinFET technology, exploring the lower limits of power consumption for a heterodyne architecture. Afterwards, a novel phase-frequency error (PFE) cancelling technique is theorized that helps to improve datarate for low power wireless communication. This technique is realized through the design of a ULP high efficiency transmitter and a ULP mixer-first receiver. Lastly, a novel low power UWB receiver architecture is defined to achieve even higher datarates than that of the PFE system. A two tone hopping technique is leveraged to meet UWB regulations while dramatically reducing required circuit power consumption.

_private_notes.value: Keywords split: "Weddings, South Asia, Urbanisation, Architecture, Industrialisation, Materiality, Class, Caste, Metropolis, Village, Countryside"

titles.title: Delhi?s Highway Wedding Venues - An Urban Form interconnecting Metropolis, Village and Countryside

abstracts.value: This dissertation explores the large-scale commercial wedding venue, a striking new multicultural building form which has emerged since the 1990s along highways at the periphery of metropolitan Delhi and other Indian villages, towns and cities. The dissertation aims at a comprehensive analysis of the wedding venue, including perspectives drawn not only from architecture but also from anthropology, as well as from the political economy of the wedding venue, where specialized entrepreneurs (tent-walas or community tent vendors, band-walas or brass bandsmen, halwais or community caterers and photographers) mobilize skilled labor guilds, usually via regional caste and family-based work and kin networks. The coordinated efforts of these actors produce wedding spectacles that combine contemporary Bollywood popular culture and tourism with elements drawn from palatial South Asian and European architectural imagery using a variety of flexible and scalable material assemblies that range among decoration, food and construction materials. Although these grand and visually impressive venues are usually associated with elaborate middle-class forms of consumption especially after the economic liberalization of 1991, research reveals that this typology also has deep roots in spatially extensive older regional metropolitan and rural endogamous caste-based wedding relations and practices. One more important concept for the dissertation is darśan—the ritual of seeing and being seen, that ties even the most contemporary wedding spectacle and associated digital visual architectural forms to traditional rites. While darśan emerges among Hindus its social structural and material manifestations manifest more secular form bundling other religious groups and urban forms as well. Finally, because these wedding venues are now almost all located along peripheral highways and regional practices of caste endogamy, the dissertation addresses issues of “highway urbanism,” especially their translation to sprawling peripheral multi-functional zones where highways and weddings define an intermediate social space between the metropolis and the village, caste and class and urbanization and industrialization.

titles.title: Genetic Associations with Cognitive Function and Neurodegenerative Biomarkers in South Asians Across India

abstracts.value: Alzheimer’s Disease (AD) and related dementias (ADRD) are a growing public health concern in India due to its large and aging population. Understanding the genetic influences on cognitive aging is imperative for addressing the burgeoning public health burden in this population. Little is known about whether dementia-associated genes and genetic variants identified in other ancestries, primarily European ancestry (EA), are associated with cognitive function or neurogenerative biomarkers for dementia in South Asians, whether genetic risk is modified by sociodemographic factors in India, or if variants specific to South Asians in India are associated with cognitive function. This dissertation examines these critical research questions in older adults from the Harmonized Diagnostic Assessment of Dementia for the Longitudinal Aging Study in India (LASI-DAD). In Aim 1, we examined whether 84 AD-associated genes from EA genome-wide association studies (GWASs) are associated with measures of cognitive function in LASI-DAD. Using an annotation score-weighted gene-based analysis, we identified three genes (APOE, TSPOAP1, PICALM) that were associated with HMSE score, general cognitive function, executive function, or orientation. These genes were previously linked to amyloid β metabolism, cholesterol metabolism, and immune system signaling. In a subsequent single-variant analysis, we identified a very rare variant enriched in South Asians compared to EA populations that was strongly predicted to be deleterious in PICALM, a gene that produces proteins needed for amyloid β precursor protein transport. In Aim 2, we examined whether genetic variants enriched in South Asians in India compared to EA, East Asian, and African populations (i.e., Indian-enriched variants) are associated with cognitive function in both the full sample and among APOE ε4 allele noncarriers by evaluating approximately 3.4 million Indian-enriched variants for associations with cognitive function. We identified several Indian-enriched variants in or near a total of 15 loci previously associated with neuropsychiatric traits, educational attainment, taurine acetylation, and cardiovascular risk factors for dementia. We also identified Indian-enriched variants in a total of 25 loci that exhibit sex-specific effects near genes previously associated with neuronal loss. All identified variants were rare/low-frequency (MAFs&l;0.05). In Aim 3, we examined whether genes previously associated with AD or neurodegenerative biomarkers in EA GWAS are associated with blood-based neurodegenerative biomarkers in South Asians. We also examined whether their effects are modified by sex and age. We performed a gene-based analysis that identified seven genes previously associated with AD, amyloid β levels, and tau levels (CLU, APOE, ICA1L, ECHDC3, EPHA1, CASS4, and CCDC6) that were also associated with neurodegenerative biomarkers in South Asians. Additionally, five genes (EPHA1, MS4A4A, MS4A6A, DGKQ, and JAZF1) had sex-specific effects on amyloid β-42, tau phosphorylated at threonine 181, and total tau, with most of the identified genes associated with lower biomarker levels in males compared to females. Finally, we also identified several genes for which genetic associations with glial fibrillary acidic protein, neurofilament light chain, and amyloid β-42 attenuated with increasing age. Many of the variants identified with the strongest associations or interactions tended to be rare/low-frequency and were predicted to be deleterious. This dissertation is among the first genetic studies of cognitive function and neurodegenerative biomarkers from a nationally representative study in India and capitalizes on the unique genetic background of those living in India. This research may help prioritize loci for further examination in risk stratification and prevention of ADRD in South Asians living in India.

titles.title: Advancing Computational Approaches to Investigate Unobserved Infectious Disease TransmissionDynamics

abstracts.value: In infectious disease epidemiology, it is often infeasible or impossible to directly collect data on important factors such as the presence of asymptomatic infection or the source of an infection. These unmeasured factors can make it difficult to understand aspects of pathogen transmission that are essential for effective intervention design, e.g., how asymptomatic infections or transmission pathways contribute to observed outbreak dynamics. In the three aims of this dissertation, I applied three distinct computational approaches to indirectly estimate factors that are hard to observe directly. The three approaches used here broadly consisted of a partially observed Markov process (POMP) model, a hybrid sampling–estimation algorithm applied to a spatial risk model, and a mechanistic model of household transmission dynamics. Using these three approaches, I examined the contribution of asymptomatic dengue transmission to the early stages of an outbreak, the association between neighborhood sanitation coverage and diarrheal risk, and the relative importance of within-household versus community-to-household transmission of Shigella and Campylobacter. In Aim 1, I used a POMP model to estimate the unobserved transmission dynamics that gave rise to observed infections from a dengue outbreak in a rural community in northwestern coastal Ecuador. The observed outbreak data left uncertainty as to when the primary case of the outbreak occurred, and, consequently, the amount of time between the initial introduction into the community and the outbreak. Here, I found that dengue could plausibly have been circulating silently in the community for as much as three months prior to the outbreak. Dengue is becoming increasingly prevalent in rural communities in South America, and this work contributes to the broader scientific literature on transmission dynamics in remote settings with immunologically naïve populations. In Aim 2, I developed a spatially informed model of diarrheal risk as a function of a new measure of neighborhood sanitation coverage, namely the Neighborhood Sanitation &a; Fecal Exposure (NSFE), examined the association between NSFE and diarrheal prevalence, and tested the potential impact of various interventions on diarrheal prevalence in counterfactual scenarios. The NSFE metric used data from rural Bangladesh, accounting for neighborhood-level characteristics including the distance to neighboring compounds and the availability and quality of latrines. I found that high neighborhood sanitation coverage was protective against diarrheal disease and that neighborhood-wide sanitation-based interventions could eliminate around 15% of diarrheal prevalence. The findings of this aim contribute to future intervention design and provide a new, spatially informed framework for quantifying neighborhood sanitation coverage. Finally, in Aim 3, I used a mechanistic model of household transmission dynamics to quantify the relative importance of within-household versus community-to-household transmission for Shigella and Campylobacter in two contexts: one urban (Bangladesh) and one rural (Tanzania). In both settings, Shigella had a higher proportion of its force of infection attributed to within the household, while Campylobacter had a stronger community-to-household transmission component. Shigella and Campylobacter combined account for a large proportion of the global burden of enteric disease, and the findings of this aim provide valuable insight into intervention design. Each aim of this dissertation provides a methodological approach tailored to elucidate important aspects of disease transmission that are difficult to directly measure in empirical studies. Our findings not only provide a methodological framework for future studies and advance our understanding of the transmission dynamics of the respective pathogens and inform effective intervention design.

titles.title: Whence Came I: On Oxxxymiron and Persona in the Digital Age

abstracts.value: This dissertation conceptualizes public persona in the digital world through the life and works of Russian rapper Miron Yanovich “Oxxxymiron” Fyodorov. It establishes that contemporary persona is an intercommunication of performance, social interaction, and mediatization, or the process of crafting public identities specifically for new media spaces. While individuals exert personal control in shaping their personas, I argue that self-presentation is a social activity. Personas are thus negotiated through and across public reception and engagement on traditional and new media platforms. Critical to my argument is how individuals use forms of “silence,” such as selective interviewing and retreats from public life, to navigate the self-presentation of their personas.The presented research is in the form of an extended case study, deconstructing and reconstructing prominent subsets of the Oxxxymiron persona: the battle rapper, the industry rapper, and the public figure. Chapter 1 serves as an introduction, orienting the dissertation’s remaining three chapters by providing a literature review in persona and Russian studies, and a limited biography of Miron Fyodorov. This information situates the ensuing case studies in an interdisciplinary framework, which contributes a new analysis of persona with a focus on the use of silence in self-presentation, into the scholarly canon. Chapter 2 presents the battle rap persona through a close reading and analysis of Oxxxymiron’s battle rap performances, paying particular attention to the social interaction of persona creation in the battle rap arena. This chapter is bolstered by scholarly research on the battle rap genre and its predecessor, an African American verbal game called the dozens. Chapter 3 presents a reading of the industry rapper persona through an analysis of the relationship between two of Oxxxymiron’s most critical releases: Gorgorod and “Who Killed Mark?” It demonstrates how the Oxxxymiron persona is negotiated through creative works, and how his musical output navigates biographical experiences as a form of persona control. Finally, Chapter 4 addresses the public figure, beginning with Oxxxymiron’s role in shaping public discourse through his discussions of national issues such as the suppression of free speech and the War in Ukraine. This chapter moves from a focus on Oxxxymiron’s speaking out to others’ breaking of silence about his private actions through their accusations of grooming and sexual assault. It considers the paradoxical roles of silence and speaking, personal fandom, and the consequences of “new public intimacy,” i.e., the role of celebrity in the evolving conceptions of public, private, and intimate spheres in the digital age.Whence Came I is a multi-disciplinary project, drawing from Russian studies, persona studies, hip hop studies, and performance studies. It puts Svetlana Boym’s concept of the “theater of the self” in conversation with Erving Goffman’s dramaturgical metaphor of self-presentation in a contemporary persona studies framework. This dissertation research will contribute to various fields by offering a nuanced and dynamic consideration of persona in the digital age. It is proliferated by presentational media and digital social interactions, demonstrating that who we pretend to be is a mediated conversation, full of pauses and rebuttals, rather than a declarative statement. This project asserts that it is not just the language that is used to curate a persona that matters, but also its absence, particularly in the restless and reactive digital age.

_private_notes.value: FAC:::Climate and Space Sciences and Engineering

titles.title: The Intersection of AI and Climate Modeling: Emulators for Physical Parameterizations and Frameworks for Testing AI Weather Models

abstracts.value: The increasing intersection of Machine Learning (ML) and Earth system modeling presents both transformative opportunities and fundamental challenges. This dissertation examines the integration of ML techniques across three critical fronts in climate and weather modeling: emulating physical parameterizations, coupling ML components into traditional general circulation models, and probing dynamical responses in fully data-driven forecasting systems.In the first part, we evaluate the skill of Random Forest (RF) and Neural Network (NN) emulators trained on a hierarchy of physics configurations within the Community Atmosphere Model (CAM). The latter is the atmospheric component of the Community Earth System Model from the National Center of Atmospheric Research. Results show that while RFs can accurately reproduce tendencies and precipitation fields in simplified settings, their performance degrades as complexity increases. We further demonstrate that by incorporating domain knowledge through targeted feature selection, we can improve emulator skill. This work underscores the importance of offline benchmarking and highlights the limitations of tree-based methods in these highly nonlinear regimes, while maintaining skill relative to simple NN benchmarks.The second part transitions from offline evaluation to online deployment, embedding both RF and NN emulators into CAM. This stage reveals practical challenges in real-time ML–physics coupling, including NN instability in particularly active regions, RF memory constraints, and interface incompatibilities between ML frameworks and CAM’s highly optimized Fortran infrastructure. These findings show that successful ML integration must address a variety of both scientific and software engineering constraints.The final project peers into the evaluation of next-generation AI-based weather forecasting systems, focusing primarily on Google’s GraphCast model. We extend the tendency reversion diagnostic framework to GraphCast’s 37-level model to assess dynamical responses to perturbations such as tropical heating anomalies. Although architectural constraints may limit full tendency reversion applicability, this work demonstrates how idealized dynamical tests can be adapted for various data-driven forecasting systems, offering a complementary perspective to traditional score-based metrics. In applying this framework, we also find that the 37-level GraphCast exhibits substantially improved representation of expected wave responses to our perturbed input compared to the 13-level operational version.Together, these studies chart a path toward responsible development, deployment, and diagnosis of ML-based Earth system models. They point to the need for architecture-aware diagnostics, scalable coupling tools, and hybrid modeling paradigms that combine data-driven flexibility with physical interpretability. As ML continues to reshape the modeling landscape, this work argues for rigorous, domain-informed testing and integration strategies that bridge modern computational approaches with the foundational principles of atmospheric science.

keywords.value: This dissertation explores machine learning approaches for Earth system modeling, grounded in foundational scientific principles.

titles.title: Domain Specific Accelerator and System-on-Chip Designs for Wireless Communication and Data Processing

abstracts.value: The exponential proliferation of wireless communication and data-intensive applications, such as Large Language Models (LLMs), has precipitated a surge in demand for computational power and energy efficiency. However, the slowdown of Dennard scaling and the deceleration of transistor miniaturization pose significant difficulties to scaling compute-intensive Integrated Circuits (ICs). Domain-Specific Architectures (DSAs) mitigate these limitations by exploiting domain-specialized dataflows and heterogeneous integration to enhance both performance and energy efficiency. This dissertation investigates DSAs across three pivotal domains: Forward Error Correction (FEC), wireless communication, and general-purpose GPU computation, while implementing optimizations across the circuit, ASIC architecture, and SoC integration levels.First, this dissertation presents two fully configurable, multi-mode FEC decoder architectures designed to maximize flexibility and throughput. The TFEC architecture unifies Turbo, Low-Density Parity-Check (LDPC), and Polar decoding within a single framework via algorithm-hardware co-optimization, realizing 25% logic and 49% memory savings while delivering throughput up to 950 Mb/s. Building upon this, the QFEC architecture extends support to Convolutional Codes (CC), integrating a compressive memory system and configurable interconnects to enable dynamic trade-offs between throughput and coding gain. Fabricated in 12 nm technology, QFEC operates at 637 MHz, achieving a theoretical throughput of up to 25.4 Gb/s.Second, to optimize wireless communication datapaths with near-ASIC efficiency, the Domain-Focused Advanced Software-Reconfigurable Heterogeneous (DASH) SoC is introduced. Engineered for a 100 mW power budget and throughput up to 1 TOp/s, DASH integrates domain-flexible ASIC accelerators with low-power ARM cores through a cooperative software-hardware optimization strategy. This framework encompasses system-level emulation, hardware schedulers, and architecture-specific programming optimizers. The DASH chip, fabricated in 12 nm, has been validated using complete wireless communication baseband protocols, supported by an accompanying FPGA emulation platform. Measured on silicon, its heterogeneous configurable architecture demonstrates an energy efficiency gain of more than one order of magnitude.Third, the dissertation introduces Razor III, a Hardware-Detection Software-Correction (HDSC) Adaptive Voltage Scaling (AVS) technique tailored for GPU architectures. Razor III minimizes energy consumption by eliminating conservative design margins and dynamically adapting circuit behavior to process, environmental, and data variations. This is achieved using customized Razor flip-flops (RFFs) for timing error detection and software checkpointing for correction. Bridging the gap between theoretical AVS benefits and physical implementation, this work contributes an automated design space exploration toolchain integrated with industrial EDA flows and a novel, low-overhead RFF topology. Implemented in TSMC 16nm FinFET technology and validated via simulations of a Razorized Vortex GPGPU, this methodology demonstrates power reductions of up to 46.3%. Furthermore, the proposed toolchain enables the systematic optimization of trade-offs among timing speculation intensity, silicon area, and voltage scaling limits.In conclusion, this dissertation advances the state of DSA hardware design through three primary contributions: the highly flexible tri-mode and quad-mode FEC decoders, the heterogeneous DASH SoC, and the HDSC Razor III AVS scheme. These works collectively address critical challenges in high-throughput ASIC architectures, large-scale heterogeneous SoC integration, and universal, non-intrusive circuit techniques. Together, they provide concrete insights and methodologies for the future of efficient wireless communication and data processing architectures.

titles.title: Evaluating and Enhancing Language Model Factuality

abstracts.value: Language Models (LMs) have become the dominant approach in natural language generation, with widespread adoption in tools such as chatbots and search engines, as well as high-stakes domains like healthcare, law, and finance. Yet LMs tend to generate inaccurate or unverifiable content, a phenomenon known as hallucination, which undermines their reliability and poses risks in real-world applications. The factuality of LMs, i.e., their adherence to established knowledge, is therefore crucial for their trustworthy deployment.This dissertation addresses the challenge of LM factuality along two key dimensions: evaluation and enhancement. Factuality evaluation is critical for identifying and characterizing hallucinations in LMs, while enhancement techniques aim to mitigate hallucinations and improve the factual accuracy of generated content.We begin by developing an efficient Open Information Extraction system that extracts factual content units from raw text. The system operates in two stages: extracting triple constituents, i.e., subject, relation, and object, then consolidating these into compact triples. While not an evaluation tool itself, this claim-level representation enables fine-grained factuality assessment in long-form text generation.Building on this, we introduce a factuality evaluation framework that includes a benchmark of diverse, real-world, and challenging user-LM interaction prompts, along with an evaluation method that extracts verifiable claims from LM outputs and categorizes their factual accuracy based on Web-retrieved evidence. Our approach demonstrates a stronger correlation with human judgments than existing long-form factuality evaluations.On the enhancement front, we propose an inference-time intervention system that steers LM generations using the model’s internal representations of truth. By analyzing model outputs across varying intervention intensities, our system selects the most accurate response when the model is confident and expresses uncertainty otherwise. To further mitigate hallucinations, we also develop a post-processing technique that extracts and verifies factual claims against external knowledge sources and revises inaccurate claims for enhanced factuality.Together, these contributions represent key steps toward improving the factuality of modern natural language generation systems. Our work facilitates the development of more trustworthy LMs, particularly in applications where factual consistency is critical.

keywords.value: AI Models&s; capability evaluation and enhancement
